Off Calendar Feb-03-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Scott Righthand, Esq. (415.544.0115; Scott@righthandlaw.net) for Plaintiff.
Brian Mooney, Esq. (510.463.8600; bmooney@grsm.com) for Defendants, Abbott et al.
Lindsey Romano, Esq (510.463.8600); lromano@grsm.com) for Defendants, Abbott et al.
Jennifer Still, Esq. (408.861.6500; jstill@hinshaw-law.com) for Defendants, Stanford Health Care, et al.

Not argued. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS STANFORD HEALTH CARE and DIPANJAN BANERJEE'S DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

Defendants' (DIPANJAN BANERJEE, M.D. and STANFORD HEALTH CARE) demurrer to the amended complaint is overruled.

Pursuant to CCP sec. 452, the court liberally construes plaintiff's amended complaint. "[M]aterial facts alleged in the complaint are treated as true for the purpose of ruling on the demurrer." (C&H Foods Co. v. Hartford Ins. Co. (1984) 163 Cal.App.3d 1055, 1062.) In addition, "[a] demurrer does not lie to a portion of a cause of action." (PH II, Inc. v. Superior Court (1995) 33 Cal.App.4th 1680, 1682.)

The court grants defendants' request for judicial notice of the official government filings. (Evidence Code sec. 452(c).) "Taking judicial notice of a document is not the same as accepting the truth of its contents or accepting a particular interpretation of its meaning". (Joslin v. H.A.S. Insurance Brokerage (1986) 184 Cal.App.3d 369, 374; see also Cruz v. County of Los Angeles (1985) 173 Cal.App.3d 1131, 1134 ["[T]aking judicial notice of the official acts of a governmental entity does not in and of itself require acceptance of the truth of factual matters which might be deduced therefrom"].) A demurrer may not be turned into a contested evidentiary hearing where the truthfulness of the judicially noticed documents is disputable. (See Silguero v. Creteguard, Inc. (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 60, 64.)

Here, plaintiffs' claims are predicated on defendants' failure to provide a proper emergency contact number to decedent as well as their role in the manufacturing, distribution, etc. of the HeartMate 3. (Amended Complaint, pars. 19-22, 217.) The products liability allegations take this case out of MICRA and a factual issue remains whether defendants had a role in the manufacture, distribution, etc. of the product or whether defendants merely provided medical services. Defendants cite numerous cases for the proposition that hospitals merely provide services and are not liable for products liability. (See, e.g., Silverhart v. Mt. Zion Hosp. (1971) 20 Cal.App.3d 1022, 1027 [explaining after trial that "[a] hospital is not ordinarily engaged in the business of selling any of the products or equipment [it] uses in providing such services. The essence of the relationship between a hospital and its patients does not relate essentially to any product or piece of equipment it uses but to the professional services it provides."].) Plaintiffs correctly note that defendants largely rely on trial/summary judgment cases and is improperly contesting the truth of plaintiffs' allegations. The demurrer is therefore overruled.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-03-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Scott Righthand, Esq. (415.544.0115; Scott@righthandlaw.net) for Plaintiff.
Brian Mooney, Esq. (510.463.8600; bmooney@grsm.com) for Defendants, Abbott et al.
Lindsey Romano, Esq (510.463.8600); lromano@grsm.com) for Defendants, Abbott et al.
Jennifer Still, Esq. (408.861.6500; jstill@hinshaw-law.com) for Defendants, Stanford Health Care, et al.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS STANFORD HEALTH CARE and DIPANJAN BANERJEE'S MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT.

Defendants' (DIPANJAN BANERJEE, M.D. and STANFORD HEALTH CARE) motion to strike is denied. Defendants assume/argue that defendants' liability is solely based on professional negligence. As explained in the companion demurrer, plaintiffs plead sufficient facts that defendants are also liable for products liability. The court takes those allegations as true when ruling on the motion to strike. (See Willis v. City of Carlsbad (2020) 48 Cal.App.5th 1104, 1121.) The theory of recovery based on products liability is not subject to CCP sec. 425.13 or MICRA. The judicially noticed documents do not conclusively show that the products liability theory of recovery fails. The motion to strike is denied since there is a factual dispute regarding defendants' precise role in providing decedent care and the HeartMate 3.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Atty. SCOTT RIGHTHAND, Esq. of Law Office of Scott Righthand (Tel: 415-544-0115) for plaintiffs;
Atty. JENNIFER STILL, Esq. of Hinshaw, Marsh, Still & Hinshaw, LLP (Tel: 408-861-6500) for defendants Stanford Health Care and Dipanjan Banerjee, M.D.;
Atty. LINDSEY MACK ROMANO, Esq. of Gordon Rees Scully Mansukhani, LLP (Tel: 510-463-8600) for specially appearing defendants Abbott Laboratories, Inc. and Abbott Laboratories, and for defendants Abbott Cardiovascular Systems, Inc., Thoratec Corporation, and Thoratec LLC.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS STANFORD HEALTH CARE and DIPANJAN BANERJEE's Motion To Change Venue And Transfer Of Action To Santa Clara County Superior Court.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Stanford Health Care and Dipanjan Banerjee's motion to change venue under CCP 395.5, 396b and 398 is denied. A "strong presumption" favors a California plaintiff's choice of forum. (National Football League v. Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 902, 926-27.) A foreign corporation - such as defendant Abbott Laboratories here - that fails to designate a principal place of business in California may be sued in any county in the state. (Rogers Dec. Ex. D; Easton v. Sup. Ct. (1970) 12 Cal.App.3d 243, 246-47.) Effectively Abbott is resident in all California counties and San Francisco is thus a proper venue. (See CCP 395(a)(venue proper "where the defendants or some of them reside").) Moving defendants do not contend that any of the opinions they cite address a corporation which failed to designate a principal place of business.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.
